course_description: Theory and practice of economic policy. Fiscal & monetary policy in open economy. Fixed versus flexible exchange rates. Economic interdependence and international business cycles. Growth and development policy. Money and inflation in Australia. Political business cycles, central bank independence and inflation targeting. Fiscal policy and inflation. Labour markets and unemployment.

requirements.assumed_background: It is assumed that students will have previously covered material in the area of Macroeconomics at the introductory level and also some study at the intermediate level e.g. a sound knowledge of the closed economy ISLM model, open economy ISLM model, AD-AS modeling framework and the Neoclassical growth model.

timetable:
Timetables are available on mySI-net.

Additional Timetable Information Tutorials for this course begin in Teaching Week 2. Students are required to sign-on for a tutorial via mySI-net. Sign-on will be open from Tuesday 19th July 10:00am until Thursday 11th August 12:00pm .

Next 2. Aims
course_aims:
The aim of this course is to further provide students with an understanding of the key relationships and causal links between well-defined major macroeconomic aggregates. The exposition of economic policy is quite general in applicability, although we take contemporary and historical examples from the Australian and the global context.

The global financial crisis challenges the theory and policy perspectives of an influential segment of the discipline whose models are based on efficient markets. Critiques from within and from (slightly) outside the mainstream will be included in this course. Also discussed are the practical problems faced by policy-makers when they are confronted with unfamiliar problems. As employers of macroeconomists value a graduate's ability to understand and even anticipate policy responses (especially changes in interest rates), we will try to get inside the heads of policy makers. This involves understanding the models that organise their thinking and looking at the facts that affect their practical judgements.

learning_outcomes.description: Command and use a core, comprehensive and well-founded knowledge of the field of modern macroeconomics.
Better understand and interpret macroeconomic indicators.
Have the ability to define and analyse problems based on models as applied in an institutional context.
Identify key contemporary macroeconomic problems, both domestic and international and discuss possible solutions and potential innovations towards improving current policies and practices.
Collect, analyse, and organise information and ideas independently through wider reading and to convey those ideas clearly and fluently.
Obtain analytical skills and capacity for lateral thinking.
Understand the theory and its assumptions so that better policy recommendations can be made

assessment_details.title: ECON2040 Mid Semester Examination
assessment_details.category: Exam - Mid Semester Outside Scheduled Class
assessment_details.task_description:
The test is on a Saturday. If the test can be scheduled for week 7 (Sept. 10), the intention is that the 90 minute exam will cover the first 5 lectures, 4 tutorials and other associated assessable reading materials.

You will be advised of the date (and therefore the exact coverage) as soon as the University Administration can finalise their scheduling and let me know. Past papers will be of some significant, though partial, use, but note that the course structure and content changed in 2010.

Learn.UQ

or
mySI-net

to access marking criteria held in this profile.

assessment_details.title: ECON2040 Final Exam
assessment_details.format: Multiple-choice, Short answer, Short essay, Problem solving
assessment_details.task_description:
The final examination is designed to cover all learning objectives and to test both depth and breadth of students knowledge relevant to the course. (The end of semester examination will be two hours in duration, plus the usual 10 minutes perusal, and will consist of material dealt with from ALL of the lectures and tutorials throughout the semester, with additional emphasis on the material covered after the mid-semester examination.)

There will be a short essay (or a set of short-answer questions) on a pre-announced topic relating to policy-setting in the Australian context. MCQs and/or short-answer questions will form the rest of the final exam. Some questions focus specifically on assigned readings.

Other questions focus more on analytical material (graphical or mathematical problem-solving). If the mid-semester test indicates areas of weakness, these may be assessed again on the final exam to encourage the class to respond to assessment feedback provided after the test.

The exam set in 2010 is the only example you have, but guidance and sample questions will be provided on Blackboard in due course.

learning_resources.required_resources: A Reading List is being compiled. An indicative list (not necessarily final) is posted on Blackboard. Some items will be statements (as simple and succinct as I can find) of how macroeconomic theory and policy needs to evolve to account for financial crises. Others will be extracts from economists who represent important (influential) theoretical viewpoints that you should be familiar with but which many textbooks often ignore. Extracts from other sources may appear in the Commentaries that are posted after my lectures.
learning_resources.recommended_resources:
Check Blackboard for overheads, tutorial questions and answer guides, additional recommended readings and practice materials etc. [Available on the ECON2040 website [http://blackboard.elearning.uq.edu.au/]

Since the GFC, events and policy debates have moved quickly. They may still be in motion. Readily accessible web resources, if any, will be listed to allow you to keep pace with developments.

http://blackboard.elearning.uq.edu.au/

Macroeconomics , 2nd edition, (2006), Rudiger Dornbusch, Philip Bodman, Mark Crosby, Stanley Fischer, & Richard Startz, McGraw‑Hill, Sydney.

This book was set in ECON2020. If you already own it, keep it as slices will be relevant. If you do not own a copy, read the (final) version of the Course Profile and judge whether to obtain a copy.

Useful supplements to this text are available on the internet at: www.mhhe.com/au/dornbusch2e.

Students enter this course with different backgrounds. Not all have studied ECON1020 at UQ in 2010 or earlier. This means that you may not be familiar with the AD model in inflation and output-gap space or with the basic principles of achieving an inflation target using the official cash rate. You may therefore need to refer sometimes to Littleboy and (John) Taylor, Macroeconomics , latest edition, Wiley.

As the lectures will be recorded on Lectopia, there is less pressure to take detailed lecture notes during lectures. (It is wise to tidy up the notes you make during a lecture shortly afterwards.)
Instead of writing detailed notes during lectures, you may gain more by listening carefully and by asking questions. (If the technology fails, Ill post more elaborate notes than I would have done otherwise.) You can use Lectopia to take more detailed notes.

"Commentaries" are posted on Blackboard after the related (BL) lecture. The lag depends on whether the lecture is newly written. They are sometimes summaries of part of the lecture, especially if there is no focused study material in the Reader or textbook I can use. They may contain study advice (concept priorities, tips etc.).

As there is a Reader (and possibly other readings given individually), watch for advice in these Commentaries. Lectures, tutorials and Blackboard announcements also can advise you about what points you need to grasp or what sections in an item are central for understanding and/or assessment. During the semester, I'll provide some questions on each item that indicate some of what is relevant for assessment. (Who says what, who disagrees with whom and why, when did this debate occur and how was it resolved...?) Commentaries are suitable places to put these.
learning_resources.university_learning_resources:
Access to required and recommended resources, plus past central exam papers, is available at the UQ Library website ( http://library.uq.edu.au/search/r?SEARCH=ECON2040 ).

The University offers a range of resources and services to support student learning. Details are available on the myServices website ( https://student.my.uq.edu.au/ ).

For assessment in this course students will be permitted to use Casio FX82 series (82 with any letters) or university approved (labelled) calculators . For further details on approved calculators http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/exam-calculators .

Calculator requirements may differ for other university courses and students should check each course profile for the specific requirements.

PLEASE NOTE: It is the students responsibility to ensure they have the correct calculator prior to assessment in each of their courses. Students without the correct calculator in a School of Economics exam may have their calculator confiscated.

Guidelines for correct referencing techniques can be found in the School of Economics publication Guide for Assignment Presentation , which can be accessed on the web site www.uq.edu.au/economics
learning_resources.other_learning_resources:
Note that much assessable material is based on materials beyond any textbook; e.g., what is discussed in the lectures, tutorials and in extra specified readings etc. (If something is not assessable, this would normally be made clear. If in doubt, ask me, the lecturer.)

Dornbusch, Bodman et al. was also used in ECON2020, Macroeconomic Theory. You have therefore already studied much of it closely. This means that other material beyond this textbook will play a significant role in the study (and assessment) of ECON2040.

Useful supplements to this textbook (including some practice materials relevant to assessment) may be available on the internet at: www.mhhe.com/au/dornbusch2e . To obtain the username and password to access the student section of these online supplementary materials, you will need a registration code, which is included on the card at the front of the textbook.

The overheads, tutorial questions and answer guides, additional recommended readings and practice materials will be available on the ECON2040 website on Blackboard in reasonably timely fashion. Extra, more detailed, supplementary materials I call "Commentaries", will be posted after some lectures. (There may be a lag because some will be written after the relevant lecture or in response to macroeconomic events.)

Previous 2. Aims

Next 4. Activities

learning_activities.topic: Information (Lecture Series): Current Lecture Schedule: Bruce Littleboy is the principal lecturer, and John Quiggin provides an early and broad overview of the macro policy debates from Keynes to the GFC in weeks 2 and 3 (August 2 and 9). Later lectures fill out the details or build upon this overview. Note: it is not yet settled whether the mid-semester exam will be held in-class or on a Saturday. If it is on a Saturday, it will be either 3rd, 10th or 17th September. The date will be known by mid August. There is no lecture on the Tuesday before the Saturday exam. This implies that this Course Profile cannot provide a complete and accurate schedule. Estimates of time for each topic may be a little inaccurate because these mostly are newly-written lectures. Don't expect a topic to fit into a single lecture. If you miss a lecture, check Blackboard and Lectopia to see where we are up to. Tutorials contain assessable material and they start in week 2.

Topic 1: Orientation (Lecture): Challenges to theory and policy posed by the GFC. The standard goals, methods and rationale. (Is the economy like a machine?) The two-way relationship between micro and macro processes. The variety of macro-dynamics. Readings/Ref: Blackboard; Reading List; Lectopia; Commentary

Topic 2a: Postwar Keynesianism (Lecture): [JQ] Post-war Keynesianism, Phillips curve, fiscal vs monetary policy debate, inflation expectations and dynamics, Friedman, rational expectations, NCE. Readings/Ref: Blackboard

Topic 2b: Theories of Policy-making before the GFC (Lecture): [JQ] Inflation and monetary policy; New Classical & RBC models, New Keynesians, convergence Lucas critique and stabilization Taylor rules and central bank independence with a focus on the Australian experience.

Topic 3: Traditional Theories of Policy Making (Lecture): Demand shocks. Rules vs discretion (intro); stop-go; lags. Instruments and targets (Tinbergen); assignment of instruments to targets; trade-offs. The political business cycle (how positive analysis differs from normative analysis. [Likely to exceed 2hrs] Readings/Ref: Blackboard (Supp notes (BB), extra reading and tutorial); Reading List

Topic 4: Deciding Monetary Policy (Lecture): Poole's rules; inflation targeting and its IS-LM depiction; deflation; forecasting via leading indicators; how central bankers think; Knut Wicksell; asset prices. [May exceed 2 hours] Readings/Ref: Blackboard; Reading List

No lecture on Tuesday: Saturday Midsemester Test (Exam): DATE AND COVERAGE SUBJECT TO CHANGE To permit preparation for the test, there is no lecture on the Tuesday prior to the test. (If the test is on the 10th, there is no lecture on the 6th.) On material from weeks 1-5 inclusive, if the test is held on week 7 or 8. See Blackboard for details of coverage and exact date, time and venue(s).

Topic 5: Theories of the Business Cycle (Lecture): Types of shocks and cycles; Keynesian, classical and other theories: Walras' Law and Clower's critique; effective demand, Patinkin's disequilibrium analysis; the forgotten aspects of Keynes. Readings/Ref: Blackboard; Reading List; Lectopia; Commentary

Topic 6: Causes of Crises (Lecture): The GFC (incorporating Open Economy issues. Causes and consequences of crises; Theories about market-driven crises (esp. Minsky, Leijonhufvud) Globalisation; Inefficient asset markets and financial markets; voluntary vs involuntary unemployment
Topic 7: Crisis Management (Lecture): Policies during crises; lessons learned? Recent restoration of the stabilisation role of fiscal policy (and "functional finance")
Topic 8: Saving and Investment (Lecture): Short-term and long-term relationships between S&I; fiscal policy Readings/Ref: Blackboard; Reading List; Lectopia; Commentary

policies_and_procedures:
University Policies & Guidelines

An overview of the Universitys assessment-related policies can be found on myAdvisor ( http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/index.html?page=2910 ).

Academic Integrity
It is the University's task to encourage ethical scholarship and to inform students and staff about the institutional standards of academic behaviour expected of them in learning, teaching and research. Students have a responsibility to maintain the highest standards of academic integrity in their work. Students must not cheat in examinations or other forms of assessment and must ensure they do not plagiarise.

Plagiarism
The University has adopted the following definition of plagiarism:

Plagiarism is the act of misrepresenting as one's own original work the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of another. These include published and unpublished documents, designs, music, sounds, images, photographs, computer codes and ideas gained through working in a group. These ideas, interpretations, words or works may be found in print and/or electronic media.

Students are encouraged to read the UQ Academic Integrity and Plagiarism policy ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25128 ) which makes a comprehensive statement about the University's approach to plagiarism, including the approved use of plagiarism detection software, the consequences of plagiarism and the principles associated with preventing plagiarism.

Applications for Extensions

Medical grounds: Applications for extension on medical grounds shall be made by lodging the Application for Extension of Progressive Assessment form and supporting documentation at the location outlined in Section 5.3 of the Electronic Course Profile for the course concerned.

Exceptional circumstances: Applications for extension on the grounds of exceptional circumstances shall be made to the relevant Course Coordinator/Program Director by lodging the Application for Extension of Progressive Assessment form and a personal statement outlining the grounds for the application at the location and by the due date outlined in Section 5.3 of the Electronic Course Profile for the course in which this application is made. If the exceptional circumstances are such that the student cannot reasonably be expected to have complied with these conditions, a case should be made as to why these conditions could not be met.

Outcome of application: Students will be advised of the outcome of their application via their student email.

Feedback on Assessment
Feedback is essential to effective learning and students can expect to receive appropriate and timely feedback on all assessment. For a detailed explanation of the feedback you are entitled to, you should consult the policy on Student Access to Feedback on Assessment. ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25109 )

As a student you have a responsibility to incorporate feedback into your learning; make use of the assessment criteria that you are given; be aware of the rules, policies and other documents related to assessment; and provide teachers with feedback on their assessment practices.

There are certain steps you can take if you feel your result does not reflect your performance. Please refer to the myAdvisor web site. ( http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/index.html?page=2953 )

School of Economics

University Policies and Guidelines

Placement Courses
Students on a placement course also known as a work placement, internship, industry study, industry experience, clinical practice, clinical placement, practical work, practicum, fieldwork, teaching practice should refer to the University policy, Placement Courses ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25120 ) for detailed information.

Working with Children
Students whose studies include a professional/work placement, internship, clinical practice, teaching practice or other similar activity which involves them in regular contact with children should refer to the University policy, Working with Children Check - "blue card" ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25004 ) to find out how to apply for a blue card.

Students with a Disability
Any student with a disability who may require alternative academic arrangements, including assessment, in the course/program is encouraged to seek advice at the commencement of the semester from a Disability Adviser at Student Support Services. Refer to the University policy, Students with a Disability (Disability Action Plan) ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25123 ) and to the policy on Special Arrangements for Examinations for Students with a Disability ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25111

While it is the responsibility of the relevant faculty to liaise with professional and registration bodies regarding the acceptability of any adjustment to an academic program, the University Health Service can arrange appropriate advice and assistance on personal and public health issues.

Occupational Health and Safety
Undergraduate Students ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25055 ) and Postgraduate Students ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25057 ) should be familiar with the University policies on occupational health and safety in the laboratory.

Previous 5. Assessment

Next Summary
course_introduction:
This course covers topics in modern macroeconomic policy at the intermediate level and builds significantly on the material covered in the required prerequisite ECON2020 Macroeconomic Theory course at this level. The core aim of this course is to provide further understanding of the key relationships and causal links between major macroeconomic aggregates. The course provides analytical and conceptual frameworks within which to evaluate policies that hope to influence, or even fine-tune, these aggregates. The exposition of economic policy in this course is often quite general ("theoretical") in nature, although effort is made to focus the course material selectively on some of the major economic issues important in the contemporary Australian and global context. We will try to see how central banks and fiscal authorities understand how economies work and how policies take effect. Policy-makers learn much from debates between academics over theory and policy.

A recurrent theme in this course is the recent revival of Keynesian-style intervention and the associated re-assessments of Keynes's economic theories. For generations there have been disagreements about theory and policy. This course aims to equip you with a wide range of perspectives to help prepare you for problems and controversies you will likely encounter during your professional life. We explore what macroeconomists agree about and disagree about. Similar debates resurface, so it is desirable to familiarise yourself with the basic arguments that recur. Some students may be unfamiliar with courses that do not offer right answers to learn. In much of this course, you will instead need to know who said what, when they said it and why they said it.

Time permitting, coverage will include: the efficient use of policy instruments to strive to achieve the targets of economic policy, time consistency of policy, microfoundations of macroeconomics, trend economic growth and policy, issues in macroeconomic stabilisation and policy effectiveness both under "normal" and under crisis conditions, the rational expectations hypothesis (its importance and its limitations), voluntary and involuntary unemployment and the peculiarities of the labour market, the resurgence of fiscal policy, tax reform, constraints imposed by budget deficits and public debt (the meaning of "sound finance"), and political and partisan cycles that affect how government policy in practice may depart from those policies most economists may recommend. There are many theories that explain the business cycle, and you will be introduced to some of them. Business cycles have various causes: policies that contain fluctuations from one set of causes may leave the economy more susceptible to other shocks. Crises are surprisingly frequent, and we still do not understand them well, or know how to deal with them or prevent them.

The trend in macroeconomics is towards understanding aggregate economic activity using models with well-defined, consistent foundations at the microeconomic level. There is a fundamental interconnection between macroeconomics and microeconomics. So some elementary concepts from microeconomics will be used in this course. Disagreements about macroeconomics often reflect disagreements about how to model the choices individuals make. A beautiful model does not always help us predict events in a messy world.

Simple algebra and elementary calculus may sometimes be used in the development and explanation of economic frameworks. Expect to see economic analysis explained graphically, mathematically and in words. Expect assessment in these three forms too. An understanding of economic models is a means of comprehending the role and probable effectiveness of economic policy. Parts of the assessment will be devoted specifically to the mathematical aspects of the course. Other parts will focus on comparing and contrasting different approaches to macroeconomic management. It more directly addresses issues of theory and policy raised by the GFC and earlier crises.

This course is not based on a textbook and a set of models with supporting technical exercises. Some topics are mathematical and formal; others are not, and assessable material will be delivered through lectures, tutorials, support materials and readings.

Course Changes in Response to Previous Student Feedback

Last year there was an emergency illness of a staff-member and teaching duties were suddenly re-arranged. The course last year was newly built week-by-week, and some students were uncertain about what they needed to know for assessment. The course this year is still a work-in progress, but this time there are more study materials, practice questions and sample test questions. Ones written during last year will be available more quickly this year. Flexible assessment weightings are preserved.

graduate_attributes.intro: Successfully completing this course will contribute to the recognition of your attainment of the following UQ (Undergrad Pass) graduate attributes:

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A1 . A comprehensive and well-founded knowledge in the field of study.

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A4 . An understanding of how other disciplines relate to the field of study.

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A5 . An international perspective on the field of study.
B1 . The ability to collect , analyse and organise information and ideas and to convey those ideas clearly and fluently , in both written and spoken forms .
B2 . The ability to interact effectively with others in order to work towards a common outcome.
B3 . The ability to select and use the appropriate level , style and means of communication .
B4 . The ability to engage effectively and appropriately with information and communication technologies .
C1 . The ability to work and learn independently .
C3 . The ability to generate ideas and adapt innovatively to changing environments.
C4 . The ability to identify problems , create solutions , innovate and improve current practices .
D1 . The ability to define and analyse problems .
D2 . The ability to apply critical reasoning to issues through independent thought and informed judgement .
D3 . The ability to evaluate opinions , make decisions and to reflect critically on the justifications for decisions.
E1 . An understanding of social and civic responsibility .
E2 . An appreciation of the philosophical and social contexts of a discipline.
E4 . A knowledge and respect of ethics and ethical standards in relation to a major area of study.
E5 . A knowledge of other cultures and times and an appreciation of cultural diversity .

other_learning_activities_information:
You are strongly encouraged to attend all of the teaching sessions. No textbook contains all the assessable material in this course or presents it in an orderly way. Notes posted on the web cannot cover everything. Failure to attend either a lecture or a tutorial may result in you being seriously disadvantaged.
As some students cannot attend lectures by necessity rather than merely by choice, I try to make lectures Lectopia-friendly, with detailed slides. But you still do lose something if you dont attend. You lose the chance to ask questions and to observe body language. If you simply prefer not to attend, you are telling yourself every week that you lack enthusiasm and interest in the course, and your attitude is typically reflected in your level of attainment. Remember that Lectopia fails sometimes.
In particular, tutorial attendance is strongly recommended as an important supplement to lecture attendance. Some tutorial questions will be basic, others advanced. You will also practice the model solving and model manipulation skills you will need to do the mid-semester test and/or final exam. Tutorials can go into extra assessable detail.
Tutorial attendance will not be used in borderline cases to make the final allocation of grades this year.

Previous 3. Resources

Next 5. Assessment
course_grading:
Grade X: No assessable work received.

Grade 1, Fail: Fails to demonstrate most or all of the basic requirements of the course:

Complete failure to demonstrate sufficient basic knowledge and understanding of key concepts, relationships and models. Awarded for an overall mark in the range 0-19.99 percent inclusive.

Grade 2, Fail: Demonstrates clear deficiencies in understanding and applying fundamental concepts; communicates information or ideas in ways that are frequently incomplete or confusing and give little attention to the conventions of the discipline:

Failure to demonstrate sufficient basic knowledge of key concepts, relationships and models. Awarded for an overall mark in the range 20-44.99 percent inclusive.

Grade 3, Fail: Demonstrates superficial or partial or faulty understanding of the fundamental concepts of the field of study and limited ability to apply these concepts; presents undeveloped or inappropriate or unsupported arguments; communicates information or ideas with lack of clarity and inconsistent adherence to the conventions of the discipline:

Some basic knowledge is evident, but detail and precision are patently absent at a level sufficient to merit competency and therefore a passing grade. Awarded for an overall mark in the range 45-49.99 percent inclusive.

Grade 4, Pass: Demonstrates adequate understanding and application of the fundamental concepts of the field of study; develops routine arguments or decisions and provides acceptable justification; communicates information and ideas adequately in terms of the conventions of the discipline:

A basic knowledge of core concepts and modelling techniques obtained. Awarded for an overall mark in the range 50-64.99 percent inclusive.

Grade 5, Credit: Demonstrates substantial understanding of fundamental concepts of the field of study and ability to apply these concepts in a variety of contexts; develops or adapts convincing arguments and provides coherent justification; communicates information and ideas clearly and fluently in terms of the conventions of the discipline:

A more thorough and accurate understanding of modelling and policy analysis displayed through both written and verbal means. Awarded for an overall mark in the range 65-74.99 percent inclusive.

Grade 6, Distinction: As for 5, with frequent evidence of originality in defining and analysing issues or problems and in creating solutions; uses a level, style and means of communication appropriate to the discipline and the audience:

A thorough, accurate and comprehensive knowledge of concepts and model analysis displayed and some evidence of wider reading. Awarded for an overall mark in the range 75-84.99 percent inclusive.

Grade 7, High Distinction: As for 6, with consistent evidence of substantial originality and insight in identifying, generating and communicating competing arguments, perspectives or problem solving approaches; critically evaluates problems, their solutions and implications:

Accuracy and attention to detail displayed in all analysis together with significant evidence of critical evaluation of material and of wider reading. Requires an overall mark of 85 percent and above.

Other Requirements & Comments:
In accordance with School of Economics policy on grades determination, percentage marks will NOT be rounded (e.g. 84.99 will remain as 84.99 and the student will be awarded a 6).
other_assessment_information:
The School of Economics is committed to reducing the incidence of plagiarism. Further information on plagiarism and how to avoid an allegation of plagiarism is available under Section 6. Policies and Guidelines - 6.1. Assessment Related Policies & Guidelines of this Course Profile.

Scheme 1: Midsemester Exam 30%, Final Exam 70%
Scheme 2: Midsemester Exam 50%, Final Exam 50%

In this course, there is flexible weighting. For the purpose of determining your final grade and reporting final scores, we will use the scheme that maximises your score and therefore your grade.

SPECIAL EXAMINATIONS

If you are unable to attend a scheduled exam (e.g. due to illness or other unavoidable circumstances which can be evidenced - e.g. medical certificate), you may be eligible to sit a special examination at a later time. Note that the mid-semester exam (test) is a SCHOOL exam, and the final exam is a CENTRAL EXAM.

Information about special examinations can be found on the web at http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/special-examinations and on the School of Economics website at

http://www.uq.edu.au/economics/policy-on-absences-from-mid-semester-examinations Please read BOTH these websites very carefully.

All applications for special exams must be submitted within (5) calendar days of the original date of the examination.

Mid-Semester Examination (SCHOOL exam) - submit your application to the Course Administrator, Christina Ellsworth, with appropriate documentation attached. Do not submit applications to the Faculty Collaborative Learning Centre or the Student Centre.

End of Semester Examination (CENTRAL exam) - submit your application to the Student Centre, Level 1, J.D. Story Building with appropriate documentation. Do not submit applications to the Course Administrator.

SUPPLEMENTARY EXAMINATIONS

If you fail a course (generally with a grade of three), you can find information about supplementary examinations at : http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/supplementary-examinations-2901 . Read this information very carefully to see if you may be eligible to apply for a supplementary examination. Also see General Award Rules, Section 1A.17.3, and your program rules.

Applications for supplementary exams must be lodged online via mySI-net, within 5 days of the official release of results.

Important Information regarding Supplementary Examinations for courses within the Faculty of Business, Economics & Law:

· Supplementary Examinations are weighted at 100% (i.e. progressive assessment does not count) and therefore ALL modules and topics are assessable.

· Results for Supplementary Examinations cannot be graded higher than a four (4).

· Once approved, applications cannot be rescinded.

· Students are advised to take the Special & Supplementary Exam period into account when making post-examination plans (14-16 December).
